sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Kayla Ward, a 7th-grade student, presents with acne on her face, which started approximately two years ago and is present most every day.
She has been using Persa-Gel and washing regularly, which has been somewhat helpful.
There are no associated symptoms, including itching, bleeding, or pain.
Her mother reports that the acne flared when she started school sports in the fall, likely due to increased sweating.
Kayla has a regular menstrual cycle, which started approximately two years ago.
She is generally a healthy child with no additional past medical history.
She lives with her parents and sister and has pets.
She plays basketball and volleyball and tap.
She uses sunscreen with SPF 30 in the summer.
Review of systems: There are no additional symptoms in other organs relevant to her initial presentation.
Past medical history: None mentioned.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The examination revealed multiple benign nevi on the trunk and extremities, scattered skin-colored papules, open and closed comedones, and erythematous papules on the face, primarily on the forehead and with some on the central cheeks and chin.
The chest and back were relatively spared.
The remainder of the examination was normal.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: Kayla Ward presents with mild to moderate acne, mostly comedonal with a small inflammatory component.
Problem list: Acne; [Status: confirmed]
Plan:
I prescribed adapalene 0.1% cream to be applied in a thin layer to the areas where Kayla generally gets acne, starting with a few nights a week and gradually increasing to every night.
I also recommended using a mild cleanser, a non-comedogenic moisturizer with sunscreen SPF 30 or higher in the morning, and a non-comedogenic moisturizer at night.
I instructed Kayla to use Persa-Gel and a non-comedogenic moisturizer after applying the adapalene.
I advised her to wear sunscreen when outside due to potential sensitivity from the adapalene.
Follow-up: Kayla will follow up in six months to assess progress.
